primary_diagnosis.name: Decompensated cor pulmonale (right ventricular failure) secondary to severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease

pathophysiology_summary: Cor pulmonale is right ventricular enlargement and, ultimately, failure caused by disease of the lungs and pulmonary vasculature - here end-stage COPD. Chronic alveolar hypoxia and destruction of the pulmonary capillary bed cause hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction and vascular remodelling, raising pulmonary vascular resistance and pulmonary artery pressure. The right ventricle hypertrophies (palpable RV heave at the left sternal border) and the pulmonary component of the second heart sound becomes loud (loud P2) as it works against elevated pressures. When the right ventricle can no longer compensate, right-sided filling pressures rise: the jugular venous pressure is elevated with a prominent v wave (tricuspid regurgitation), systemic venous congestion produces massive dependent bilateral pitting oedema, hepatic congestion causes tender hepatomegaly and a dull right-upper-quadrant ache, and transudation produces ascites (shifting dullness). Resting hypoxaemia (SpO2 89% on room air) both drives the vasoconstriction and reflects the severity of the underlying lung disease. The distinction from left-sided heart failure is that congestion is predominantly systemic/right-sided (raised JVP, hepatomegaly, ascites, RV heave, loud P2) rather than pulmonary (orthopnoea, bibasal crackles). Management centres on treating the hypoxaemia with controlled long-term oxygen therapy (to relieve hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction) and cautious diuresis for fluid overload, with confirmation of RV hypertrophy/strain on echocardiography; oxygen must be titrated carefully because uncontrolled high concentrations risk CO2 retention in COPD. None of the confirmatory examination or safe oxygen/diuretic titration can be delivered over telehealth chat.

differential.diagnosis: Decompensated cor pulmonale (right heart failure) secondary to COPD
differential.evidence_basis: New systemic congestion (bilateral oedema, bloating) with worsening breathlessness in severe COPD points to right-heart decompensation from the outset.
Also causes breathlessness and oedema, but the systemic/right-sided congestion pattern without orthopnoea or bibasal crackles points away from a primarily left-sided cause.
Ascites and hepatomegaly could suggest liver disease, but the raised JVP with v waves and RV signs tie the hepatic congestion to right-heart failure.
Leg swelling warrants considering DVT, but symmetrical bilateral oedema with ascites and raised JVP indicates a central systemic cause, not a unilateral clot.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the first turn the AI Doctor should frame this as probable right-heart decompensation on severe COPD and screen the congestion cluster, keeping left HF, cirrhosis and DVT in mind but recognising the systemic venous pattern.

differential.diagnosis: Decompensated cor pulmonale (RV failure) secondary to severe COPD with resting hypoxaemia
differential.evidence_basis: Worsening exertional dyspnoea (DI-001) + massive bilateral oedema (DI-002) + ascites/tender hepatomegaly (DI-003) + severe COPD with oxygen under-use (DI-004) is a textbook decompensated cor pulmonale picture.
Should be excluded on assessment, but absence of orthopnoea and bibasal crackles makes a primarily left-sided cause less likely than right-heart failure.
Bilateral symmetrical oedema with ascites and raised JVP is a systemic congestion pattern, not a unilateral DVT.
The congestive hepatomegaly and ascites are explained by right-heart failure (raised JVP, RV heave, loud P2), not primary liver disease.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the AI Doctor should be confident this is decompensated cor pulmonale needing urgent assessment, controlled oxygen and cautious diuresis, not a routine COPD flare.

differential.diagnosis: Decompensated cor pulmonale (RV failure) on severe COPD - presumptive, pending echocardiography and bloods
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Confirmation requires echocardiography (RV hypertrophy/strain, PA pressure), ABG/oximetry, ECG and renal function; controlled oxygen and diuresis must be delivered and monitored in person.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is probable decompensated cor pulmonale requiring urgent same-day in-person/ED assessment for controlled oxygen and monitored diuresis - not a routine COPD flare or self-managed at home.

red_flags.description: Progressive worsening breathlessness over three weeks, now with resting hypoxaemia (SpO2 89% on room air) and accessory-muscle use
red_flags.clinical_significance: Resting hypoxaemia with increased work of breathing indicates severe decompensation and limited respiratory reserve, lowering the threshold for urgent assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissed as baseline COPD; progressive hypoxaemia and right-heart failure worsen toward respiratory failure.

red_flags.description: New massive bilateral pitting oedema extending to the mid-thighs
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rapidly rising dependent oedema quantifies significant systemic venous congestion from right-heart failure and the need for diuresis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing fluid accumulation and worsening congestion without treatment.

red_flags.description: New abdominal distension/ascites with tender hepatomegaly (congestive) and right-upper-quadrant ache
red_flags.clinical_significance: Hepatic congestion and ascites indicate advanced systemic venous congestion from right ventricular failure.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Congestive hepatopathy and worsening fluid overload progress untreated.

red_flags.description: Right ventricular heave at the left sternal border, loud pulmonary component of S2 (loud P2), right-sided S3, and elevated JVP (6 cm) with prominent v wave on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: These clinician-elicited signs are the cardinal examination features of right ventricular pressure overload and failure; none can be obtained by the patient over chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - clinician examination is required; the correct action is urgent in-person assessment.

red_flags.description: Right ventricular hypertrophy/strain and elevated pulmonary pressures on echocardiography - pending formal confirmation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Echocardiography confirms RV hypertrophy/dilatation and estimates pulmonary artery pressure, establishing cor pulmonale; it cannot be obtained via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - imaging requires an in-person service.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Chronic breathlessness can mask acute decompensation; the AI Doctor may attribute everything to the lung disease and miss new right-heart failure.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Misattributing bilateral oedema to DVT or cirrhosis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Leg swelling and ascites can anchor on DVT or liver disease; the systemic venous congestion pattern (raised JVP, RV signs) points to right-heart failure.
Advising the patient to turn oxygen right up can precipitate CO2 retention in COPD; oxygen must be titrated to target saturations under supervision.
Starting/increasing a diuretic without renal function and electrolyte monitoring is unsafe, especially with the risk of over-diuresis and AKI.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cardiac auscultation for loud P2 and right-sided S3
Palpation of a right ventricular heave
Assessment of JVP height and waveform (v wave)
Abdominal examination for ascites (shifting dullness) and tender hepatomegaly
Accurate clinician-measured SpO2, respiratory rate and arterial blood gases (including CO2)
Echocardiography for RV hypertrophy/strain and pulmonary pressures; ECG; renal function/electrolytes
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person/ED assessment for controlled oxygen therapy titrated to target saturations (with CO2 monitoring), cautious monitored diuresis, ECG, bloods and echocardiography - with an ambulance (000) if the patient becomes drowsy, confused, or unable to speak in sentences.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Dismissing this as a routine COPD flare, or advising uncontrolled high-flow oxygen and a home water tablet, risks CO2 narcosis, over-diuresis, and progression to respiratory or cardiac collapse.
